summary:
The Qin Dynasty (221–206 BC) was the first unified Chinese empire — the political culmination of the [[event:1111|Warring States Period]], when the state of Qin under [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]] conquered all six rival states and established a single centralized imperial state over the Chinese world. Although lasting only 15 years, the Qin dynasty established the administrative, legal, and territorial framework that all subsequent Chinese dynasties built upon, making it one of the most consequential brief dynasties in world history.

Qin's success was rooted in decades of Legalist administrative reform under Lord Shang and subsequent ministers, which had created the most efficient bureaucratic, taxation, and military system among the Warring States. [[event:1119|Qin Shi Huang Unification]] completed the conquests between 230 and 221 BC. The new empire implemented sweeping standardization: [[event:1125|Qin Standardization of Measures]] unified weights, measures, coinage, and script; roads and canals (including [[event:1126|Lingqu Canal Construction]]) connected the empire; and the [[event:1121|Great Wall of China Qin]] was begun. The [[event:1122|Burning of Books and Burying Scholars]] suppressed intellectual dissent.

The dynasty's collapse was as rapid as its rise. [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]]'s death in 210 BC was followed by succession struggles, popular rebellions, and the [[event:1123|Fall of Qin Dynasty]] by 206 BC. The [[event:1124|Chu Han Contention]] settled who would succeed the Qin, resulting in the Han dynasty. Yet the Qin legacy was permanent: China's name in many foreign languages (China, Chine, Cina) derives from Qin, and the administrative model of centralized prefectural-county governance that Qin established became the template for two millennia of Chinese imperial rule.
